howItWorks.intro: This salary after tax calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: FICA: Social Security 6.2% (up to $168,600) + Medicare 1.45% (+0.9% over $200k)
Take-Home = Gross Salary − Federal Tax − State Tax − FICA
howItWorks.outro: Tax laws and financial markets change frequently. Verify current rates with your financial institution.

faq.question: How much is 100k salary after taxes?
faq.answer: A $100,000 salary in a no-income-tax state like Texas or Florida yields approximately $78,000-$80,000 take-home pay after federal income tax and FICA. In high-tax states like California or New York, take-home drops to roughly $70,000-$73,000. Your filing status significantly impacts the number, with married filing jointly paying less federal tax due to wider brackets.

faq.question: Which states have no income tax?
faq.answer: Nine states have no state income tax: Alaska, Florida, Nevada, New Hampshire, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Washington, and Wyoming. New Hampshire historically taxed only interest and dividend income, but phased that out completely in 2025. Living in a no-tax state can save you thousands annually, though other taxes (property, sales) may be higher to compensate.

faq.question: What is the difference between marginal and effective tax rate?
faq.answer: Your marginal tax rate is the bracket your last dollar of income falls into — for example, 22% for a single filer earning $75,000. Your effective tax rate is the actual percentage of total income paid in taxes, which is always lower because of progressive brackets and the standard deduction. Someone in the 22% bracket typically has an effective rate of 12-16%.

faq.question: What is FICA and why is it separate?
faq.answer: FICA stands for Federal Insurance Contributions Act and funds Social Security and Medicare. It is 7.65% of your wages (6.2% Social Security + 1.45% Medicare), and your employer pays an equal 7.65%. Social Security tax stops at $168,600 in earnings for 2024, but Medicare has no cap and adds a 0.9% surcharge on earnings above $200,000.

faq.question: How can I reduce my tax burden legally?
faq.answer: The most effective strategies include: maximizing 401k contributions ($23,000 limit for 2024) which reduces taxable income dollar-for-dollar, contributing to an HSA if you have a high-deductible health plan ($4,150 individual / $8,300 family), using traditional IRA deductions if eligible, and harvesting investment losses to offset gains. These strategies can save thousands per year in taxes.

contentIntro: Your salary offer and your take-home pay are two very different numbers. A $75,000 salary in California leaves you roughly $55,000 after federal tax, state tax, Social Security, and Medicare. That same salary in Texas leaves you about $60,000 thanks to zero state income tax. This calculator shows your actual paycheck amount after all deductions, broken down by pay period. Understanding your net pay is essential for budgeting, negotiating raises, and comparing job offers across different states. Many people are shocked to discover that a $10,000 raise only translates to $6,500-$7,500 in additional take-home pay depending on their tax bracket.
howToSteps: Enter your annual gross salary or hourly rate with expected hours
Select your state for accurate state income tax calculations
Choose your filing status — single, married filing jointly, or head of household
Enter any pre-tax deductions like 401(k) contributions or health insurance premiums
Review your net pay broken down by pay period — annual, monthly, biweekly, and weekly
tipsAndConsiderations: Pre-tax 401(k) contributions reduce your taxable income dollar-for-dollar. Contributing $500/month to a 401(k) does not reduce your paycheck by $500 — it reduces it by roughly $350-$400.
State income tax varies from 0% in nine states to over 13% in California's top bracket. This can mean a $5K-$10K annual difference on the same salary.
Overtime pay is taxed at your marginal rate, not a special higher rate. The common myth that overtime 'isn't worth it because of taxes' is false.
When comparing job offers, always convert to net hourly pay. A $90K salary with 60-hour weeks often pays less per hour than an $70K job at 40 hours.
